expectations.text: A test list or plan of test cases is created before any test code
expectations.evidence: No test list comment or planning step in strutil_test.go. Tests start directly at line 9 with TestTruncate_NoTruncationWhenWithinMaxLen. No mention of planning in transcript.

expectations.text: Test file (strutil_test.go) is created before implementation file (strutil.go)
expectations.evidence: Transcript shows 'Creating detailed failing tests' (msg 8) before 'Implementing basic word wrap' (msg 13). Test file was written first.

expectations.text: Tests use Arrange-Act-Assert structure with clear sections
expectations.evidence: Tests are one-liner assertions with no separation. Example: TestTruncate_NoTruncationWhenWithinMaxLen (lines 9-12) has two inline assertions: assert.Equal(t, 'hello', Truncate('hello', 10)). No arrange/act separation — call and assertion are combined.

expectations.text: At least 5 distinct test cases for Truncate covering degenerate, happy, and edge cases
expectations.evidence: Only 3 Truncate test functions. TestTruncate_NoTruncationWhenWithinMaxLen, TestTruncate_TruncatesAndAddsEllipsis (1 case), TestTruncate_ZeroOrNegativeMaxLenReturnsEmptyString.

expectations.text: At least 4 distinct test cases for WordWrap covering degenerate, happy, and edge cases
expectations.evidence: Only 3 WordWrap test functions: WrapsAtWordBoundaries, DoesNotSplitSingleLongWord, EmptyInputReturnsEmptyString.

expectations.text: Each test function tests exactly one behavior (no multi-assertion mega-tests)
expectations.evidence: TestTruncate_NoTruncationWhenWithinMaxLen has 2 assertions (lines 10-11) testing the same behavior with different inputs. TestTruncate_ZeroOrNegativeMaxLenReturnsEmptyString also has 2 assertions (lines 19-20).

expectations.text: Test names are descriptive sentences (e.g., TestTruncate_ReturnsOriginalWhenShorterThanLimit)
expectations.evidence: Names are descriptive: TestTruncate_NoTruncationWhenWithinMaxLen, TestWordWrap_EmptyInputReturnsEmptyString, etc.

expectations.text: Implementation uses minimal code to pass tests (no over-engineering)
expectations.evidence: Implementation has a correctness bug: Truncate returns s[:maxLen] + '...' (line 14), meaning Truncate('hello world', 5) returns 'hello...' (8 chars) — exceeds maxLen. The test expected this wrong behavior, suggesting tests didn't drive correct design.

expectations.text: Tests are run and confirmed failing before implementation is written
expectations.evidence: Transcript shows 'Running initial tests' (msg 9) and 'Running tests to catch undefined functions' (msg 11) before implementation.

expectations.text: All tests pass after implementation is complete

expectations.evidence: ParseFloat handles integers correctly. Same reasoning as with_skill.

expectations.text: A regression test for the specific bug is written BEFORE any fix is applied

expectations.text: The regression test is run and confirmed to FAIL before the fix

expectations.text: The fix uses strconv.ParseFloat instead of strconv.Atoi (as the bug description suggests)
expectations.evidence: duration.go line 39: num, err := strconv.ParseFloat(numStr, 64) — correctly replaced strconv.Atoi.

expectations.text: The fix is minimal — only changes what's needed to handle fractional values
expectations.evidence: Fix changes: (1) strconv.Atoi → strconv.ParseFloat, (2) time.Duration(num) * time.Hour → time.Duration(num * float64(time.Hour)). Same pattern for m and s cases. No other changes.

expectations.text: Existing behaviors are preserved (integer durations like '2h' still work)
expectations.evidence: strconv.ParseFloat handles integer strings (e.g., '2') correctly, returning 2.0. The math is identical for whole numbers.

expectations.evidence: Only one test: TestParseDuration_FractionalHours for '1.5h'. No tests for fractional minutes, fractional seconds, or other fractional hour values like '2.25h'.

expectations.text: All tests pass after the fix

notes: Eval 1 (string utils) shows the largest skill delta: with_skill 80% vs old_skill 40%. The test list, AAA structure, and single-behavior-per-test guidance drove measurably better test quality.
Eval 1 old_skill produced a CORRECTNESS BUG: Truncate returns s[:maxLen] + '...' (exceeds maxLen). The new skill's test decomposition guidance led to correct implementation.
Eval 2 (bugfix) is NON-DISCRIMINATING: both configs score 87.5%. The bugfix scenario is too constrained — both skills produce identical outputs. Consider removing or replacing this eval.

Eval 3 (legacy handler) is TIED at 66.7% for both configs. The Legacy Code and Test List sections in the new skill did NOT change agent behavior — characterization tests and test lists were skipped by both.

'Additional edge cases' assertion fails 100% across both configs in eval 2 — agents consistently stop after the primary regression test. The 'Bugfix TDD' section may need stronger language about testing related edge cases.

'Characterization tests first' assertion fails 100% across both configs in eval 3 — the Legacy Code section isn't strong enough to override the agent's default behavior of jumping straight to new tests.
No timing or token data available — subagent metrics were not captured. Future runs should save timing.json.
